name: Big Diggins Mine
short_description: Fee dig site in Luna County featuring turquoise and copper minerals in desert terrain. Great for collectors seeking authentic southwestern specimens at a modest cost.
description:
Big Diggins Mine is a pay-to-dig location in Luna County's high desert, offering rockhounders direct access to turquoise and associated copper mineral deposits. The site sits at 4,444 feet elevation on relatively flat terrain, making it accessible to most visitors despite the southwestern heat.

The mineralization here reflects Luna County's rich copper mining heritage. During the late 1800s and early 1900s, major copper mining operations dominated the region, and smaller deposits like this one were worked by individual prospectors. Turquoise formed in the oxidized zones of these copper ore bodies, where acidic surface waters percolated through copper-rich rock and deposited silica and copper minerals in fractures and cavities.

Visitors can expect to find small to medium turquoise specimens, often with the matrix rock attached. Chrysocolla and malachite commonly appear alongside turquoise in these deposits. The digging itself requires basic hand tools and patience - the ore is often moderately hard, requiring a pick or small pry bar to extract clean specimens.

Because this is a managed fee site, you keep what you find within the site's terms. The flat terrain and day-visit nature make it suitable for families with older children and casual collectors. Bring plenty of water and plan for intense afternoon sun in summer months.
history: Luna County was a major copper mining region during the late 1800s and early 1900s. Turquoise formed in the oxidized weathering zones of copper ore bodies where slightly acidic groundwater dissolved and redeposited copper and silica minerals, creating the turquoise and chrysocolla deposits found here today.
tips: Search the worked areas and mine tailings where previous digging has exposed fresh rock - turquoise often appears in small seams within harder host rock. Bring a small hammer and chisel or rock pick to work the ore gently and extract specimens without damaging them. Look for blue-green discoloration in the lighter-colored host rock, and examine the weathered surfaces of excavated material where turquoise is most visible. Work during morning hours to avoid peak afternoon heat and watch for unstable overhangs in worked areas.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4,444 feet in the Chihuahuan Desert transition zone. Relatively flat terrain with scattered desert vegetation including creosote, mesquite, and yucca. Hard-packed caliche and sandy soil with exposed mineralized rock. Walking distance from parking typically under 0.5 miles. Footing is uneven with loose rocks and rubble from previous mining activity.
best_season: October - April. Daytime temperatures in summer exceed 95F regularly, making digging uncomfortable and dehydration a serious risk. Winter months offer pleasant 60-75F daytime temperatures ideal for sustained fieldwork. Even in winter, bring significant water as the desert air is very dry.
written_directions: From Deming, take US 180 east toward Hillsboro for approximately 18 miles. Watch for local road signs or ask at Deming rockhounding shops for precise turn locations, as mine access roads are often unmarked. Follow directions to the specific dig area parking. The site is on private managed land with a clearly marked entrance.
road_conditions: Improved dirt road to parking area. High-clearance passenger vehicles recommended but not required. Road may be rough after rain but passable for standard vehicles in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Parking area near dig site entrance. Limited capacity - expect space for 5-10 vehicles. Parking is dirt/caliche surface.
collection_rules: This is a fee dig site. Specific rules regarding daily limits, specimen size restrictions, and what is included in the fee should be confirmed directly with the operator. Generally, you keep what you find within the terms of entry. No commercial collection or resale permitted. Respect all posted signs and site boundaries.
quantity_limits: Confirm with operator at time of visit - typically keep what you find.
rules:
No commercial collection or resale
Keep within marked dig area boundaries
Respect all posted signage
Confirm daily collection limits with operator
No motorized excavation without permission
Clean up your workspace before leaving
nearest_services: Deming, NM approximately 18 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available. Several grocery stores and restaurants.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Loose rocks and uneven mining rubble require sturdy footwear and careful footing. Not wheelchair accessible due to rough terrain and lack of paved paths. Digging requires bending and hand tool use. The heat is the primary challenge - afternoon temperatures in summer make work difficult.

faq.question: What types of turquoise can you find at Big Diggins Mine in New Mexico?
faq.answer: Big Diggins produces small to medium turquoise specimens, often with host rock attached. You will also find chrysocolla and malachite - the three minerals commonly occur together in copper deposit oxidation zones. Turquoise here typically appears as blue-green staining or small veins within lighter colored rock matrix.

faq.question: How much does it cost to dig at Big Diggins Mine in Luna County?
faq.answer: Big Diggins is a fee dig site where you pay for access and keep what you find. Contact the site operator directly for current fee information, as pricing may vary seasonally. Generally, fee dig sites in this region charge modest daily rates ($15-30 range) but confirm before visiting.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Big Diggins Mine near Deming?
faq.answer: No permit is required - it is a managed fee dig site. You simply pay the entry fee at the gate or with the operator. A waiver or agreement form may be required. No special permits, licenses, or advance registration is needed for casual collecting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to dig turquoise at Big Diggins Mine?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 95F, making sustained digging uncomfortable and dehydration a serious risk. Winter months offer pleasant 60-75F days perfect for fieldwork. Afternoon shade is scarce, so morning digging is recommended year-round.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Big Diggins?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer and chisel, rock pick, and pry bar to work the moderately hard ore. A hand lens helps identify turquoise in matrix rock. Essential: lots of water (at least 2-3 liters per person), sun protection (hat, sunscreen), sturdy boots with ankle support for rocky ground, and work gloves. A bucket or bag for specimens is helpful.

faq.question: Is Big Diggins Mine beginner-friendly for first-time rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes - the flat terrain, accessible location near Deming, and direct turquoise deposits make it excellent for beginners and families with children 8+. The straightforward fee structure and keep-what-you-find system require no special knowledge. Start with basic tools and expect a fun introductory experience to southwestern mineral collecting.

meta_title: Big Diggins Mine Rockhounding - Luna County, NM
meta_description: Find turquoise, chrysocolla, and malachite at Big Diggins fee dig near Deming. Easy desert rockhounding for families and beginners. Keep what you find.
